content: spawn_agent is non-blocking: it returns { status: 'accepted', spawnId } immediately, and the child session runs as a background Promise. This enables parallelism — coordinators call spawn_agent N times and all N children run concurrently. Blocking spawn was rejected because it would serialize all child work, preventing a coordinator from dispatching a full wave of tasks at once. The non-blocking pattern is adapted from OpenClaw's sessions_spawn, where LLMs were shown to handle 'accepted' returns correctly when the prompt contract is clear.

Coordinator agents use a wave-and-wait loop for parallel work: (1) call spawn_agent once per ready task — each returns 'accepted' immediately; (2) summarize what was spawned in the turn response; (3) the spawner's multi-turn loop calls session.prompt() with each child's completion message as it arrives; (4) the coordinator processes each completion turn: verifies task done status, handles failures; (5) if more tasks are now unblocked, spawn the next wave and repeat. The loop exits when activeCount() == 0. This pattern lives in agent-spawner.ts and activates only when the spawn tracker has registered children — sessions without spawns are unaffected.

Completion messages injected into parent sessions follow this exact format: "[spawn_completion] spawnId=<id> role=<role> outcome=<success|failed> summary=<brief text>". This is a protocol — the spawner's formatCompletionMessage() helper, the coordinator.md delegation section, and spawning.md capability doc all use this verbatim string format. The [spawn_completion] prefix lets the coordinator distinguish completion turns from normal user messages. Do not change the format without updating all three locations.

The spawner (agent-spawner.ts) and spawn tool (spawn-tool.ts) share state through a module-level Map<sessionId, SpawnTracker> managed by getOrCreateTracker() and removeTracker() in spawn-tracker.ts. The spawner calls getOrCreateTracker(sessionId) before session.prompt() so the tracker exists before any tool calls can register children; the spawn tool calls getOrCreateTracker(parentSessionId) from inside the tool handler to register each new child. This decouples the two components without a direct import dependency and is safe for concurrent multi-session use because the map is keyed by session ID.

The parent coordinator session must stay alive until all child completions have been received. If the spawner's multi-turn loop exits early (session throws, is disposed, or the caller cancels before activeCount() == 0), background child Promises continue running but their completion events are never consumed — they become orphaned. There is no crash recovery for in-process spawns. removeTracker() must be called in a finally block to clean up the module-level registry, but this does not cancel running children.

LLM agents default to treating tool return values as results. Without explicit prompt instructions, a coordinator receiving { status: 'accepted', spawnId } from spawn_agent will interpret 'accepted' as the child's outcome and move on — never waiting for actual completions. coordinator.md and spawning.md are the primary guardrails; they must explicitly state that spawn_agent is non-blocking and that results arrive later as [spawn_completion] messages. When extending or replacing these files, preserve the non-blocking contract language or coordinators will silently break.

Two resource bounds govern parallel spawning: (1) a semaphore caps simultaneous active child sessions at maxConcurrentSpawns (default 5, from spawn-limits.ts) to prevent saturating API rate limits — new spawns queue on acquire() rather than fail; (2) maxSpawnDepth (default 2) prevents recursive fan-out — coordinator (depth 0) spawns worker (depth 1), worker cannot spawn further by default. The semaphore uses FIFO queuing: acquire() pushes a resolve callback; release() shifts the next waiter and calls it directly so the slot stays consumed without an extra tick.

Parallel workers that touch overlapping files simultaneously will cause data loss or merge conflicts. There is no file locking or sandboxing. The only mitigation is task design: coordinator.md instructs workers to operate on independent files, and tasks should be scoped to non-overlapping file sets. This is an accepted architectural limitation — conflict resolution is deferred to a future sandboxing or merge layer. When designing task waves for the coordinator, file independence is a first-class constraint, not an afterthought.

The spawner's multi-turn loop calls nextCompletion(), which awaits the message bus. A hung child blocks the parent indefinitely. Per-spawn timeouts are required: on timeout, all still-running spawns are failed via tracker.fail() in a batch (runningSpawns() enumerates them), and failure completion messages are returned together. The dangling nextCompletion() Promise resolves benignly on the first fail() call since the bus fires immediately. Without this, one stuck child session can hang the entire coordinator.

Completion results are delivered to the parent by the spawner calling session.prompt() directly, not via pi.sendUserMessage({ deliverAs: 'followUp' }) from inside the extension. The pi.sendUserMessage approach requires an active Pi event loop running after session.prompt() resolves — the timing window is unreliable. Keeping the spawner in control (it calls session.prompt() for each completion) gives a clear, predictable lifecycle: prompt → completion loop → prompt → loop → done → dispose. The spawner passes a MessageBus instance through PiSpawnerOptions; callers that don't provide one get a fresh default bus.
